questions.question: Why is it recommended to use objects (rather than individual primitive values) as Workflow parameters in Temporal?

choices.text: Objects are the only parameter type that Temporal's serialization layer can handle correctly.
Using objects lets you add or change fields later without breaking the Workflow's method signature.
Primitive values cannot be made deterministic inside a Workflow, so they must be wrapped in objects.
Objects automatically compress the data sent to the Temporal server, reducing storage costs.

questions.explanation: Temporal Workflows are long-lived and their definitions evolve over time. If you pass individual primitive arguments and later need to add a new input field, you'd have to change the method signature — potentially breaking compatibility with already-running Workflow Executions. Wrapping inputs in an object means you can add new optional fields without changing the signature, making versioning much smoother.

questions.question: What does resetting a Workflow Execution do in Temporal?

choices.text: It restarts the Workflow from the very beginning, discarding all prior Event History entirely.
It pauses the Workflow and waits for an operator to manually replay each event one by one.
It stops the current Workflow Execution and starts a new one that replays history up to a chosen event, then continues from there with the current code.
It rolls back the Temporal server's database to a previous snapshot so the Workflow can re-run from that state.

questions.explanation: A Workflow reset is useful when a Workflow is stuck due to a non-deterministic bug you've since fixed. Temporal copies the Event History up to the reset point into a new Execution and resumes from there using your updated code. Any progress made after the reset point is discarded. This is a targeted recovery tool — not a full restart — so you only lose the work done after the problematic event.

questions.question: What is a Dynamic Workflow in Temporal, and when is it invoked?

choices.text: A Workflow that scales its concurrency automatically based on current Task Queue load.
A Workflow that runs on a schedule and is triggered dynamically by a cron expression at runtime.
A Workflow invoked at runtime when no other registered Workflow matches the requested Workflow name.
A Workflow that can change its own definition mid-execution in response to incoming Signals.

questions.explanation: A Dynamic Workflow acts as a catch-all handler on a Worker. If a client starts a Workflow Execution with a name that no specifically registered Workflow matches, Temporal routes it to the Dynamic Workflow instead. This is useful for building generic dispatchers or handling workflow names that aren't known at compile time. Only one Dynamic Workflow can be registered per Worker.

questions.question: What happens to a Workflow Execution when a non-deterministic error is detected and you use the Workflow Reset feature?

choices.text: The entire Event History is deleted and the Workflow restarts from scratch with the current code, discarding all previously recorded events
The current Workflow Execution is terminated, a new Execution is started with Event History copied up to the chosen reset point, and the Workflow resumes from there using the current code
The Workflow is paused at the point of the error and resumes automatically once the server detects that the non-deterministic code has been deployed away
The Workflow is replayed from the beginning using the original registered code version, and a new Workflow Task is scheduled at the earliest safe checkpoint

questions.explanation: Reset terminates the current Workflow Execution and creates a new one, copying the Event History up to the chosen event ID. The Workflow then resumes from that point using the current (fixed) code. Progress after the reset point is discarded, which is why you should only reset after fixing the underlying issue. Option A is wrong because history is preserved up to the reset point, not deleted. Option B is wrong because Temporal doesn't auto-detect or auto-resume from non-determinism. Option D is wrong because reset uses the current code version, not the original.

questions.question: After a bug is discovered in a workflow's business logic, an engineer wants to restart execution from a specific point in history rather than from the beginning. They use `temporal workflow reset --event-id 4`. What happens to workflow history events that occurred after event ID 4, and what prerequisite must be met for the reset to succeed long-term?

choices.text: Events after the reset point are preserved in a side branch of history so the workflow can merge them back later; no code change is required because the workflow replays through those events using the existing logic.

Events after the reset point are discarded, history up to event ID 4 is copied to the new execution, and the workflow resumes from that point with current code. The reset will only succeed sustainably if the underlying issue — such as non-deterministic code — has been fixed; otherwise the workflow will fail again at the same point.

The reset rewinds the workflow clock but keeps all activity results cached, so activities that already completed will not be re-executed; the prerequisite is that the worker must be restarted after the reset to clear the sticky execution cache.

Events after the reset point are archived to the namespace's archival storage and can be retrieved later; the reset itself requires no code change because Temporal automatically skips non-deterministic commands during replay.

questions.explanation: When you reset a workflow execution, Temporal copies the event history up to the specified reset point into a new workflow execution and discards all events that occurred after that point. The workflow then resumes from the reset point using the current deployed code. Any progress made after the reset point — completed activities, signals received, timers fired — is lost. Critically, the documentation states that 'reset only works if you've fixed the underlying issue, such as removing non-deterministic code.' If the bug is not fixed, the workflow will encounter the same failure again when it replays to the equivalent point in the new execution.
